As said, it's worth running those checks with your VPN and the IPv6 leak — but it's also worth running them again in the future, because prefixes get reassigned, VPN apps get updated, and routers get factory-reset. Related What the heck happened to IPv6? We were promised a new beginning. Posts 11 By Gavin Phillips IP Address Network Tips Follow Followed Like Share Facebook X WhatsApp Threads Bluesky LinkedIn Reddit Flipboard Copy link Email Close

item.title: The World Runs on Belief, Not Truth
item.description: Reality has been getting away with some very suspicious behavior.I'll continue the investigation.Subscribe if you'd like access to the case files. We like to…
item.content:encoded: The World Runs on Belief, Not Truth Truth does not threaten beliefs. It threatens everything those beliefs are protecting Pelle Martens Jul 05, 2026 154 82 65 Share Reality has been getting away with some very suspicious behavior. I'll continue the investigation. Subscribe if you'd like access to the case files. Subscribe We like to think society runs on truth. It doesn’t. There is a simple way to find out what any society truly values. Don’t listen to what it says. Watch what it protects when those two things come into conflict. Most values sound absolute when they’re never tested. Freedom matters. Justice matters. Security matters. Truth matters. For a while. Until choosing one means compromising another. The first compromise almost never feels like surrender. It feels responsible. Reasonable. Necessary. That’s exactly why almost nobody recognizes it as a compromise. They believe they’re saving more than they’re giving up. A belief does not need to be true to be useful. Sometimes it only needs to keep a family together, protect an institution, preserve a reputation, calm a population, or allow people to wake up in the morning without questioning the entire structure of their lives. This is why truth is so often treated as a threat rather than a correction. It does not simply challenge what people think. It challenges what their lives have been built around. Most people aren’t defending their beliefs. They’re defending everything those beliefs are holding together. The most unsettling truths are rarely the ones that question one belief. They are the ones that make people wonder what else might be wrong. Sometimes the first crack is frightening only because nobody knows how far it reaches. Ideas are surprisingly easy to replace. Identities are not. The longer a belief survives, the more of yourself gathers around it. By the time truth arrives, it is no longer asking you to rethink an opinion. It is asking you to rethink yourself. Society loves people who tell the truth about yesterday. It builds statues for them. Writes books about them. Teaches children to admire them. Telling the truth about today is a very different story. Suddenly you’re difficult. Divisive. Dangerous. It’s remarkable how many heroes only become heroes after they’ve stopped making everyone uncomfortable. The script almost never changes. First it calls them offensive. Then it calls them exaggerated. Then it calls them obvious. Once an idea becomes impossible to deny, everyone suddenly acts as though it had been self-evident from the beginning. Apparently the only thing shorter than public memory is public accountability. Every civilization develops an immune system. Not against disease. Against ideas. The moment an idea threatens the structure holding society together, the question changes. People stop asking whether it is true. They begin asking whether society can afford it to be true. The most effective way to defeat an idea is not to prove it wrong. It is to convince everyone the cost of believing it is too high. The greatest victories over truth rarely happen in debates. They happen long before the debate begins. They happen when certain questions start feeling inappropriate, irresponsible, or simply not worth asking. By then, the argument has already been settled without anyone noticing who settled it. The most successful societies rarely censor ideas. They simply raise the price of believing them. Careers become harder. Friendships become awkward. Reputations become fragile. Before long, people aren’t choosing between truth and falsehood. They’re choosing between truth and belonging. Belonging wins far more often than anyone likes to admit. Once a belief becomes familiar enough, people stop experiencing it as a belief. It simply becomes reality. From that moment on, every new idea carries the burden of proof. Propaganda does not tell people what to think. It teaches them which thoughts feel unacceptable before they have even finished thinking them. Once that happens, the censor no longer lives in the institution. It lives in the individual. Nothing becomes more difficult to question than the thoughts that feel entirely your own. Once the censor lives inside the individual, it no longer needs to force anything. Self-preservation does the work voluntarily. Intelligence does not make people harder to fool. It often makes them harder to convince they’re being fooled. The mind is remarkably good at explaining why what we already need to believe must somehow be true. Intelligence simply gives it better arguments. Every institution begins by serving an idea. Given enough time, it begins serving itself. Success gives it something it never had before: something to lose. From that moment on, truth is no longer the priority. Reality has never shared our confusion. People build magnificent theories about the world. Civilizations organize themselves around those theories for generations. They defend them, institutionalize them, and sometimes punish anyone who points out the cracks. Every lie eventually reaches a point where keeping it alive requires more effort than admitting the truth. More explanations. More exceptions. More people protecting it. Reality asks for none of it. That is why truth almost never wins in a single moment. Reality never needed to win. It remains exactly what it was before anyone believed anything about it. The only thing that changes is the price people pay for discovering it too late. Subscribe Share The Bigger Picture If this has earned a coffee, I’ll happily drink it. Either way, I’m glad you’re here. If you’re still here, I probably don’t need to convince you. Subscribe Share The Bigger Picture This is part of The Bigger Picture, an ongoing body of original work. © 2025 Pelle Martens. All rights reserved. 154 82 65 Share

item.title: Trump praises Allah, threatens to obliterate Iran
item.description: News Politics New threats come amid reports the regime in Tehran is targeting the U.S. president for assassination. Copy Link Copied Share via email Share on X…
item.content:encoded: U.S. President Donald Trump said that the American military stood ready to “completely decimate and destroy all areas of Iran” in response to threats to his life. Trump made the post on Truth Social in the early hours of Saturday morning. Media reports indicate that Trump received intelligence briefings from Israeli authorities that the Iranian regime was targeting him for assassination. In the post, the president said that “1000 Missiles are Locked and Loaded and aimed at the Islamic Republic of Iran” should the regime attempt to assassinate him. The new threats come amid a breakdown in a ceasefire between Iran and the U.S., which was signed in June. The U.S. launched new strikes on Iran earlier this week in response to Iran’s attacks on shipping in the critical waterway of the Strait of Hormuz. On Wednesday, Trump told reporters that Iran was plotting to assassinate him. “They want to take out the U.S. leader — me,” he said. “I’m on every list. I saw this morning, I’m on every single one of their lists. And so far, I guess I’ve been a little bit lucky, but that maybe doesn’t last very long.” However, talks between Washington and Tehran are ongoing despite the recent hostilities. “PRAISE BE TO ALLAH!” added the U.S. president, signing off his social media post.
